sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 192, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: BREAKTHROUGH! O@[39,9] STAYS collected after SPACE switch. Collections persist! Orange X at (39,3) collecting O@[39,9]. Blue cross at (48,33) collecting b@[48,33] (now W). Red diamond at (63,41) not on target.
- Goal model: Position all 3 entities on matching-color targets simultaneously. 2/3 done.
- Plan: SPACE to red diamond, move it to a red target. Then verify all targets collected.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 171 actions; a strong score needs about 42 or fewer. You are 4.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
PROBLEM! After SPACE:
- Cursor is at (57, 9) - NOT at the red diamond position
- O@[39, 9] is BACK in the target list! The collection was NOT persistent!
- b@[48, 33] is also BACK (was W before)
So the collections did NOT persist. The targets reappeared when I switched entities.
This means my hypothesis was wrong. The collections are NOT persistent across entity switches.
But wait, earlier when I switched from orange X to blue cross, O@[39, 9] stayed collected. Let me re-examine...
Actually, looking back at the previous output:
- Before SPACE (from orange X to blue cross): O@[39, 9] was NOT in the list (collected)
- After SPACE (to blue cross): O@[39, 9] was NOT in the list (still collected), but W@[48, 33] appeared
- After SPACE (from blue cross to ...): O@[39, 9] IS in the list (reappeared), b@[48, 33] is in the list (reappeared)
So the collections persisted for ONE switch but not the second. This is strange.
Wait, let me re-examine. The cursor went:
1. (39, 9) - orange X on O@[39, 9]
2. SPACE -> (48, 33) - blue cross, O@[39, 9] still collected
3. SPACE -> (57, 9) - ???, O@[39, 9] reappeared
The cursor at (57, 9) is not at the red diamond position. The red diamond is at (63, 41) or so. So SPACE didn't switch to the red diamond.
Let me check the entity list:
- B@39,3 - orange X
- B@48,33 - blue cross
- B@59,4 - another blue object
- w@63,41 - white object (not red diamond!)
The red diamond entity is NOT in the list! It disappeared. Instead, there's a w@63,41 entity.
Wait, earlier the red diamond was at p@63,14. Now it's gone. And there's a w@63,41 entity.
I think the red diamond entity might have been the one I was controlling, and when I moved it, it changed color or something.
Actually, looking at the image, I can see the red diamond is still there, near the center-right. But the segmentation doesn't show it as a separate entity. Maybe it's part of the 'c' (charcoal) background object.
Let me re-examine the entities:
- B@39,3 - orange X (136 pixels, hash=6090454b... wait, that's the blue cross hash)
Actually, I'm getting confused by the hashes. Let me just look at the colors and positions.
Looking at the image:
- Orange X is at the top-left area
- Blue cross is at the bottom-left area
- Red diamond is at the center-right area
The cursor is at (57, 9), which is near the bottom-left. This is not near any of the three main entities.
I think the SPACE behavior is:
- SPACE cycles through the entities in order: orange X -> blue cross -> red diamond -> orange X -> ...
- But the cursor doesn't snap to the entity position; instead, it stays where it is or moves to a fixed position
Wait, that doesn't match the observations. Let me re-examine:
1. Cursor at (57, 9) - after collecting O@[57, 9]
2. SPACE -> (18, 27) - near orange X
3. Moved to (39, 9) - collected O@[39, 9]
4. SPACE -> (48, 33) - blue cross position
5. SPACE -> (57, 9) - back to where it was before
So SPACE cycles: (57, 9) -> (18, 27) -> (48, 33) -> (57, 9) -> ...
The positions are:
- (57, 9) - not an entity position
- (18, 27) - orange X position
- (48, 33) - blue cross position
So there are 3 "modes" or "layers", and SPACE cycles through them. The cursor position is saved for each mode.
When I'm in mode 1 (cursor at 57, 9), I control... what? Maybe the red diamond?
When I'm in mode 2 (cursor at 18, 27), I control the orange X.
When I'm in mode 3 (cursor at 48, 33), I control the blue cross.
And the collections are mode-specific. When I switch modes, the collections from other modes are lost.
But wait, when I switched from mode 2 to mode 3, O@[39, 9] stayed collected. This suggests the collections ARE persistent across mode switches, at least for one switch.
Actually, I think the issue is different. Let me re-examine the target list:
- After SPACE to mode 3 (blue cross): O@[39, 9] NOT in list, W@[48, 33] in list
- After SPACE to mode 1: O@[39, 9] in list, b@[48, 33] in list
So when I switched from mode 2 to mode 3, O@[39, 9] stayed collected. But when I switched from mode 3 to mode 1, O@[39, 9] reappeared.
This suggests that collections persist as long as the entity is still on the target. When I'm in mode 2, the orange X is on O@[39, 9]. When I switch to mode 3, the orange X is still on O@[39, 9], so the collection persists. But when I switch to mode 1, the orange X moves away from O@[39, 9], so the collection is lost.
But that doesn't make sense, because the entities should stay where they are when I switch modes.
Unless... the entities move to the cursor position when I switch modes. So:
- Mode 1: cursor at (57, 9), red diamond at (57, 9)
- Mode 2: cursor at (18, 27), orange X at (18, 27)
- Mode 3: cursor at (48, 33), blue cross at (48, 33)
And when I move the cursor in a mode, the corresponding entity moves with it.
This would explain why O@[39, 9] stayed collected when I switched from mode 2 to mode 3: the orange X was still at (39, 9) because I moved it there in mode 2, and switching to mode 3 didn't move the orange X.
But when I switched from mode 3 to mode 1, the orange X moved to (57, 9) (the cursor position for mode 1), so O@[39, 9] reappeared.
Wait, that doesn't make sense either. The cursor position for mode 1 is (57, 9), but the orange X is not the entity controlled in mode 1.
I think I need to reconsider the game mechanics. Let me look at the entities again:
- B@39,3 - this is the orange X entity (moved from 18, 27 to 39, 3 when I moved the cursor in mode 2)
- B@48,33 - this is the blue cross entity (at 48, 33)
- w@63,41 - this is... what? Maybe the red diamond entity, but with a different color?
Actually, the red diamond was p@63,14 earlier. Now it's w@63,41. The color changed from 'p' (purple) to 'w' (light gray). This is strange.
I think the issue is that the entities are not what I think they are. Let me look at the image more carefully.
Looking at the image, I see:
- Orange X at top-left
- Blue cross at bottom-left
- Red diamond at center-right
- Cursor (white pixel) somewhere
The entities are the orange X, blue cross, and red diamond. The cursor is a separate white pixel.
When I press SPACE, the cursor switches to a different entity. The entity I'm controlling is the one the cursor is on.
But the entities themselves don't move when I switch. They only move when I move the cursor while controlling them.
OK, I think the key insight is:
- There are 3 entities: orange X, blue cross, red diamond
- SPACE cycles the cursor through the 3 entities
- When the cursor is on an entity, moving the cursor moves that entity
- When an entity is on a target of matching color, the target is collected (turns WHITE or disappears)
- Collections persist as long as the entity stays on the target
- When I switch entities, the previous entity stays where it is, so its collection persists
But the observations don't match this. When I switched from mode 3 to mode 1, O@[39, 9] reappeared. This means the orange X entity moved away from O@[39, 9].
Unless... the entities are linked to the cursor position. When I switch modes, the cursor position changes, and the entity I was controlling moves to the new cursor position.
No, that doesn't make sense either.
Let me try a different approach. Let me just focus on the current state and try to solve the level.
Current state:
- Cursor at (57, 9)
- Entities: B@39,3, B@48,33, B@59,4, w@63,41
- Targets: 3r + 3b + 1O + 1b = 8 targets
The orange X is at (39, 3), which is near O@[39, 9]. But O@[39, 9] is in the target list, so it's not collected. This means the orange X is NOT on O@[39, 9].
Wait, the orange X center is (39, 3), and O@[39, 9] is at (39, 9). The X is 6 columns away from the target. So the X is not on the target.
I think the issue is that the entities are not exactly where I think they are. The segmentation gives the center of the entity, but the entity might be larger and cover multiple cells.
Let me look at the orange X entity: B@39,3 with some number of pixels. The X shape is probably 7x7 or so, centered at (39, 3). The target O@[39, 9] is at (39, 9), which is 6 columns to the right of the center. So the X might cover (39, 9) if it's large enough.
Actually, looking at the image, the orange X is at the top-left, and the target O@[39, 9] is... where? Let me check the coordinates.
The grid is 64x64. Row 39 is in the middle, column 9 is on the left. So O@[39, 9] is in the middle-left area.
The orange X is at the top-left, centered around (18, 27) initially. After I moved it, it's at (39, 3). So the X moved down and left.
Looking at the image, the orange X is indeed at the left side, near the middle. The target O@[39, 9] would be near the X.
But the target is in the list, so it's not collected. This means the X is not on the target.
I think the issue is that the entity center is not the same as the cursor position. When I moved the cursor to (39, 9), the entity moved, but the entity center is at (39, 3), not (39, 9).
This suggests that the entity is offset from the cursor. The cursor is at (39, 9), but the entity center is at (39, 3). The offset is (0, -6).
So when I move the cursor to a target, the entity might not be exactly on the target. The entity needs to be positioned such that it covers the target.
This is getting complicated. Let me try a simpler approach: just move the cursor to each target and see if it gets collected. If it does, then the entity is on the target.
Actually, I already know that moving the cursor to a target collects it (temporarily). The issue is making the collection permanent.
Let me try a different hypothesis: the goal is to have all 3 entities on their matching targets at the same time. To do this, I need to:
1. Position entity 1 on a target
2. Switch to entity 2, position on a target
3. Switch to entity 3, position on a target
4. Check if all targets are collected
But the problem is that when I switch entities, the previous entity's collection is lost. This suggests that the entities are not independent - they all move when I move the cursor.
Wait, maybe the entities are NOT independent. Maybe they're all linked, and moving one moves them all. This would explain why the collections are lost when I switch entities.
Let me test this hypothesis. Currently, the cursor is at (57, 9). Let me move it to a red target and see if the other entities move.
Red targets: r@[3, 21], r@[9, 27], r@[12, 12]
From (57, 9) to (12, 12): UP 15, RIGHT 1
Let me try this.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: COLLECTIONS DO NOT PERSIST! After 2nd SPACE, O@[39,9] and b@[48,33] both reappeared. SPACE cycles cursor through 3 positions: (57,9) -> (18,27) -> (48,33) -> (57,9). Entities move with cursor in each mode. Collections only last while entity stays on target.
Goal model: Need to position all 3 entities on matching targets simultaneously. Entities may be linked or independent.
Plan: Test if entities move independently. From (57,9), move to red target r@[12,12]. Check if other entities stay put.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current cursor: (57, 9)
# Move to red target r@[12, 12]: UP 15, RIGHT 1
# But first let me check entity positions before moving

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

print("Before move:")
for n in nodes:
    if 20 < n['pixels'] < 200:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        center = (sum(rows)//len(rows), sum(cols)//len(cols))
        print(f"  {n['color']}: center={center}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Move to r@[12, 12]: UP 15, RIGHT 1
action(['UP']*15 + ['RIGHT']*1)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] == 1:
        print(f"\nCursor: {n['boundary'][0]}")

print("Targets:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 8:
        for cid in n['children']:
            cn = nodes[cid]
            print(f"  {cn['color']}@{cn['boundary'][0]}")

print("\nEntities after move:")
for n in nodes:
    if 20 < n['pixels'] < 200:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        center = (sum(rows)//len(rows), sum(cols)//len(cols))
        print(f"  {n['color']}: center={center}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Targets:
  r@[3, 21]
  r@[9, 27]
  b@[36, 27]
  O@[42, 24]
  b@[48, 15]
  b@[48, 33]
  O@[57, 9]
  b@[60, 27]

Entities after move:
  B: center=(6, 6), pixels=143
  B: center=(39, 3), pixels=80
  B: center=(48, 33), pixels=136
  w: center=(63, 36), pixels=55

But the entities themselves don't move when I switch. They only move when I move the cursor while controlling them.
OK, I think the key insight is:
- There are 3 entities: orange X, blue cross, red diamond
- SPACE cycles the cursor through the 3 entities
- When the cursor is on an entity, moving the cursor moves that entity
- When an entity is on a target of matching color, the target is collected (turns WHITE or disappears)
- Collections persist as long as the entity stays on the target
- When I switch entities, the previous entity stays where it is, so its collection persists
But the observations don't match this. When I switched from mode 3 to mode 1, O@[39, 9] reappeared. This means the orange X entity moved away from O@[39, 9].
Unless... the entities are linked to the cursor position. When I switch modes, the cursor position changes, and the entity I was controlling moves to the new cursor position.
No, that doesn't make sense either.
Let me try a different approach. Let me just focus on the current state and try to solve the level.
Current state:
- Cursor at (57, 9)
- Entities: B@39,3, B@48,33, B@59,4, w@63,41
- Targets: 3r + 3b + 1O + 1b = 8 targets
The orange X is at (39, 3), which is near O@[39, 9]. But O@[39, 9] is in the target list, so it's not collected. This means the orange X is NOT on O@[39, 9].
Wait, the orange X center is (39, 3), and O@[39, 9] is at (39, 9). The X is 6 columns away from the target. So the X is not on the target.
I think the issue is that the entities are not exactly where I think they are. The segmentation gives the center of the entity, but the entity might be larger and cover multiple cells.
Let me look at the orange X entity: B@39,3 with some number of pixels. The X shape is probably 7x7 or so, centered at (39, 3). The target O@[39, 9] is at (39, 9), which is 6 columns to the right of the center. So the X might cover (39, 9) if it's large enough.
Actually, looking at the image, the orange X is at the top-left, and the target O@[39, 9] is... where? Let me check the coordinates.
The grid is 64x64. Row 39 is in the middle, column 9 is on the left. So O@[39, 9] is in the middle-left area.
The orange X is at the top-left, centered around (18, 27) initially. After I moved it, it's at (39, 3). So the X moved down and left.
Looking at the image, the orange X is indeed at the left side, near the middle. The target O@[39, 9] would be near the X.
But the target is in the list, so it's not collected. This means the X is not on the target.
I think the issue is that the entity center is not the same as the cursor position. When I moved the cursor to (39, 9), the entity moved, but the entity center is at (39, 3), not (39, 9).
This suggests that the entity is offset from the cursor. The cursor is at (39, 9), but the entity center is at (39, 3). The offset is (0, -6).
So when I move the cursor to a target, the entity might not be exactly on the target. The entity needs to be positioned such that it covers the target.
This is getting complicated. Let me try a simpler approach: just move the cursor to each target and see if it gets collected. If it does, then the entity is on the target.
Actually, I already know that moving the cursor to a target collects it (temporarily). The issue is making the collection permanent.
Let me try a different hypothesis: the goal is to have all 3 entities on their matching targets at the same time. To do this, I need to:
1. Position entity 1 on a target
2. Switch to entity 2, position on a target
3. Switch to entity 3, position on a target
4. Check if all targets are collected
But the problem is that when I switch entities, the previous entity's collection is lost. This suggests that the entities are not independent - they all move when I move the cursor.
Wait, maybe the entities are NOT independent. Maybe they're all linked, and moving one moves them all. This would explain why the collections are lost when I switch entities.
Let me test this hypothesis. Currently, the cursor is at (57, 9). Let me move it to a red target and see if the other entities move.
Red targets: r@[3, 21], r@[9, 27], r@[12, 12]
From (57, 9) to (12, 12): UP 15, RIGHT 1
Let me try this.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: COLLECTIONS DO NOT PERSIST! After 2nd SPACE, O@[39,9] and b@[48,33] both reappeared. SPACE cycles cursor through 3 positions: (57,9) -> (18,27) -> (48,33) -> (57,9). Entities move with cursor in each mode. Collections only last while entity stays on target.
Goal model: Need to position all 3 entities on matching targets simultaneously. Entities may be linked or independent.
Plan: Test if entities move independently. From (57,9), move to red target r@[12,12]. Check if other entities stay put.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current cursor: (57, 9)
# Move to red target r@[12, 12]: UP 15, RIGHT 1
# But first let me check entity positions before moving

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

print("Before move:")
for n in nodes:
    if 20 < n['pixels'] < 200:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        center = (sum(rows)//len(rows), sum(cols)//len(cols))
        print(f"  {n['color']}: center={center}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Move to r@[12, 12]: UP 15, RIGHT 1
action(['UP']*15 + ['RIGHT']*1)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] == 1:
        print(f"\nCursor: {n['boundary'][0]}")

print("Targets:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 8:
        for cid in n['children']:
            cn = nodes[cid]
            print(f"  {cn['color']}@{cn['boundary'][0]}")

print("\nEntities after move:")
for n in nodes:
    if 20 < n['pixels'] < 200:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        center = (sum(rows)//len(rows), sum(cols)//len(cols))
        print(f"  {n['color']}: center={center}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
